text: Among novice runners, Type B (noncompetitive) behavior is a significant predictor of running-related injury, with an 11.9% higher cumulative injury risk after 500 km of running compared to Type A behavior.
specific_value: 11.9% cumulative injury risk difference after 500 km

text: Novice runners aged 45-65 years have a 14.7% higher cumulative injury risk after 500 km of running compared to younger runners, though this was borderline significant.
Previous injuries not related to running are associated with an 11.1% higher cumulative injury risk after 500 km in novice runners.
BMI greater than 30 kg/m² is associated with increased running-related injury risk in novice runners, while BMI below 20 kg/m² is protective.
specific_value: BMI >30 increased risk, BMI <20 protective, p=0.06

text: Sex, previous running-related injury, running experience, and participation in other sports activities are not significant predictors of injury among novice runners.
Among 930 novice runners, 27.3% sustained a running-related injury during a 1-year follow-up covering 155,318 total km of running.
Previous running-related injuries do not predict future running-related injuries in novice runners, contrary to findings in experienced runner populations.
